title: Inviscid Limits From Compressible Navier-Stokes to Small BV Solutions to Euler
speaker: Alexis Vasseur
abstract:
The compressible Euler equation can lead to the emergence of shock discontinuities in finite time, notably observed behind supersonic planes. A very natural way to justify these singularities involves studying solutions as inviscid limits of Navier-Stokes solutions with evanescent viscosities. The mathematical study of this problem is however very diﬃcult because of the destabilization eﬀect of the viscosities.
Bianchini and Bressan proved the inviscid limit to small BV solutions using the so-called artificial viscosities in 2004. However, until very recently, achieving this limit with physical viscosities remained an open question.
In this presentation, we will provide the basic ideas of classical mathematical theories to compressible fluid mechanics and introduce the recent method of a-contraction with shifts. This method is employed to describe the physical inviscid limit in the context of the barotropic Euler equation, and to solve the Bianchini and Bressan conjecture in this special case. This is a joint work with Geng Chen and Moon-Jin Kang.
tags: Joint IAS/PU Analysis and Mathematical Physics

proof_stages.label: Barotropic Euler/Navier-Stokes inviscid-limit problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates the one-dimensional compressible barotropic Euler system and its density-dependent-viscosity Navier-Stokes approximation, identifies the energy as a convex entropy, and states the main convergence theorem for small-BV Euler data and strongly convergent Navier-Stokes initial data.

proof_stages.label: Known compactness and vanishing-viscosity gap
proof_stages.summary: Scalar Kruzkov theory, small-BV front-tracking uniqueness, compensated compactness, and the Bianchini-Bressan artificial-viscosity theorem are reviewed to isolate the missing result: convergence from the physical compressible Navier-Stokes approximation to the unique small-BV Euler solution.

proof_stages.label: Relative entropy as the stability mechanism
proof_stages.summary: The central method is recast as relative or modulated entropy: smooth limiting solutions are strongly stable against very weak perturbations, and relative entropy to a constant is itself an entropy controlling an L2-type distance.

proof_stages.label: Shifted and weighted contraction for shocks
proof_stages.summary: After reducing local small-BV behavior to constants, rarefactions, and shocks, the failure of ordinary L2 contraction at shocks is repaired by allowing a time-dependent spatial shift and, for systems, fixed weights so that a relative-entropy distance decreases across a shock.

proof_stages.label: Euler small-BV stability via shifted front tracking
proof_stages.summary: A front-tracking approximation of the small-BV Euler solution is shifted according to the rough comparison solution, with weights reconstructed at wave interactions, thereby controlling the non-BV component while the standard Bressan theory controls the BV component.

proof_stages.label: Uniform stability of viscous shock layers
proof_stages.summary: Viscous shock layers create the new Navier-Stokes difficulty, so the proof requires contraction estimates uniform in the viscosity, valid after rescaling for very large perturbations and using viscosity-growth bounds to compensate for the lack of uniform L-infinity control.

proof_stages.label: Layered front tracking and compactness passage to the limit
proof_stages.summary: For a fixed Euler front-tracking scale one chooses much smaller viscosity, replaces fronts by viscous layers during free flight, lets close fronts interact at the Euler level, reconstructs layers and weights, and finally combines the resulting non-BV control with compensated compactness to identify the strong limit as the unique small-BV Euler solution.

examples.name: Scalar Kruzkov contraction
examples.note: The scalar conservation-law case, where relative entropies to constants yield L1 contraction, is used as the motivating template for the system-level L2/relative-entropy strategy.
examples.engagement_reason: he describes the scalar Kruzkov relative entropy comparison to constants and explains how integrating it yields an L1 contraction

examples.name: Burgers shock with a small left-state perturbation
examples.note: The perturbed Burgers shock shows explicitly that a tiny L2 perturbation can create an order-epsilon shock displacement, revealing why unshifted L2 contraction fails and why orbital shifts are needed.
examples.engagement_reason: he computes for a Burgers shock with left state 1+epsilon that the shock shifts at speed epsilon/2 and compares the resulting L2 and L1 errors

examples.name: Full Euler system and contact discontinuity
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker explains how the shifted-contraction structure extends to the 3-by-3/full Euler setting for extreme shocks and special contact discontinuities, while the Navier-Stokes limit faces additional density/temperature bounds issues.
examples.engagement_reason: in the questions he states that full Euler/contact discontinuities have a special structure involving temperature but does not derive or check it
